title: SIPs, Index Funds & the Magic of Compounding
summary: Investing regularly through a SIP lets compounding work over years. Starting early usually beats starting big later. The key levers are time in the market, consistency, and keeping costs low.

sections.heading: What a SIP is
sections.body: A SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) invests a fixed amount every month into a fund — often a low-cost index fund. It automates the habit and averages your buy price over time.

sections.heading: Why compounding rewards early starters
sections.body: Returns earn their own returns. Because of this, money invested earlier has more years to grow, so starting a small SIP early can outgrow a larger SIP started years later.

sections.heading: Risk and time horizon
sections.body: Markets move up and down in the short term. Money you'll need within a few years shouldn't be in volatile investments; long-horizon money can ride out the bumps.

quiz.prompt: The main advantage of starting a SIP early is:
options: More years for compounding to work

quiz.prompt: Money you'll need next year is best kept:
options: In volatile equity for higher returns

quiz.prompt: What does a SIP actually do?
options: Invests a fixed amount every month, averaging your buy price over time
Guarantees a fixed return each month
Locks your money for a fixed five-year term
Your returns are paid out and spent each year
Returns earn their own returns over time
Markets never fall in the short term

quiz.prompt: The lesson highlights index funds mainly because they are:
options: A way to beat the market every year
Low-cost, which helps keep more of your returns

disclaimer: Educational content only. No specific fund or product is recommended. Consult a SEBI-registered adviser for personalized advice.
